title: Repairing cracked floor tiles without replacing them
subtitle: Fill and colour-match cracks in ceramic or porcelain floor tiles
excerpt: How to repair hairline and moderate cracks in ceramic or porcelain floor tiles using epoxy filler and tile paint or coloured grout, without removing and replacing the tile. Covers when in-situ repair is viable, filling technique, colour matching, and when only replacement will do.
sourceNotes: BS 5385-1: Wall and floor tiling. Tile Association technical guidance (open-access). Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics.

glossaryTerms.definition: A two-part adhesive and filler consisting of a resin and a hardener. When mixed, it sets to a rigid, waterproof, non-shrink surface. Used for filling cracks and chips in tiles, baths, and sinks. Available tinted in white, grey, ivory, and beige; can be colour-matched with universal pigment.

content.text: A cracked tile is worth repairing in place when the crack is superficial (hairline, extending only through the glaze and not through the body of the tile) and the tile is still bonded firmly to the floor. If the tile sounds hollow when tapped, the crack has let moisture in and the adhesive bond has failed, the tile must come out and be replaced. The same applies to a tile cracked across more than a third of its face; the structural integrity is compromised and the tile will fracture further under foot traffic. For superficial cracks, the repair described here is invisible on close inspection and robust.

in a colour close to the tile body (white, grey, buff, or black). Universal pigment paste for tinting. Acetone or methylated spirits for cleaning. Fine-grit sandpaper (400 grit wet-and-dry). Clear floor varnish or tile sealer (optional, for a gloss finish over the repair).
Step 1, clean and prepare the crack

Clean along the crack with a small stiff brush to remove all loose glaze chips, dirt, and grease. Wipe the crack with a cloth dampened with acetone to degrease the surface; any grease will prevent the epoxy from bonding. Allow to dry completely. Apply masking tape on each side of the crack, leaving the crack itself exposed; this keeps the epoxy off the undamaged glaze surface.
Step 2, mix and apply the epoxy

Mix equal parts of the two-part epoxy filler on a clean board and add a small amount of pigment to match the tile colour. Work quickly; most two-part epoxy fillers have a working time of 5 to 10 minutes at room temperature. Press the mixed filler into the crack with the filling knife, forcing it below the glaze surface level slightly. The filler will shrink slightly as it sets; overfilling by 0.5 mm compensates for this.

Allow the epoxy to cure fully, typically 4 to 8 hours at room temperature; longer in cold conditions. Remove the masking tape once the filler is firm but not yet fully hard. Wet-sand the set repair with 400-grit paper until it is flush with the surrounding glaze surface. A final wipe with acetone restores a slight sheen. For a higher-gloss finish, apply one thin coat of clear floor varnish over the repair and allow to cure before use.

For cracks in unglazed stone or terracotta tiles, a matching coloured grout can be pressed into the crack with a finger and wiped flush before it sets. Unsanded grout works better than sanded for hairline cracks. This is the fastest repair for natural stone and does not require mixing or curing time.

attrs.symptom: The epoxy repair debonds after a few months
attrs.cause: Grease or moisture was present in the crack when the epoxy was applied.
attrs.fix: Rake out the old filler. Degrease and dry the crack thoroughly, including allowing the floor to dry fully from beneath if any moisture is present. Re-apply.

attrs.symptom: The crack is too wide (more than 1 mm) for epoxy filler alone
attrs.cause: Major tile body crack; epoxy alone may not bridge it reliably under traffic.
attrs.fix: Fill the body of the crack with a one-part sealant first, allow to cure, then apply the epoxy over the top to match the glaze level. The sealant bridging layer takes the movement; the epoxy layer provides the surface appearance.
